GTM Snapshot  /  The CMO Game
Advanced Marketing  ·  Brian D Marr  ·  Maitri K.

Go-to-Market
Snapshot:
The CMO Game

I developed this GTM snapshot after playing The CMO Game in Prof. Marr's Advanced Marketing class, where Andrew Mitrak came to the class in person. I used Claude to help build and stress-test the strategy, then revised where AI got it wrong.

Product
cmogame.com
Creator
Andrew Mitrak
Launched
Feb 2026
Price
Free, No Login
01Product Overview

The CMO Game is a free browser-based marketing strategy simulator with a retro pixel-art aesthetic that immediately signals: this is a game, not a training module. You play as Director of Marketing with a $5M annual budget and 12 months to reach the C-suite. You pick a product, lock in a positioning strategy, build your team, and make monthly budget decisions while managing CEO pressure, brand equity, and audience reactions.

Monthly budget allocation across Brand Building, Performance Marketing, PR and Influencers, and Events. Prior decisions compound: underinvest in brand early and the CEO starts asking questions by Month 4.

Unexpected events hit mid-game, from industry conferences to PR crises, and your prior spending decisions affect the outcome. A full playthrough takes about 15 minutes. A Hall of Fame leaderboard ranks the top 100 players globally, replacing the need for a completion certificate: the rank is the credential.

Mid-game unexpected event: the Skincare Symposium expo. Prior spending on Events unlocks bonus samples. This is where the game stops feeling like a simulator.
Customer Problem
Marketing is widely taught through lectures, frameworks, and case studies. None of these simulate the organizational pressure of managing budgets under CEO scrutiny or defending brand equity decisions. The CMO Game fills this gap by putting learners inside those decisions without real career consequences, at no cost.
Category and Competition
Marketing strategy simulator. Complements Harvard Business Publishing simulations (expensive and institutional) and passive formats like podcasts and books. One of the few free, frictionless, decision-under-pressure options in this category.
02Target Audience and Customer Insight

When I finished playing, my instinct was to share it with my MBA classmates entering marketing roles. That reaction is itself a data point about who this product is for.

Audience Profile
Age 24 to 32. In or recently graduated from an MBA or graduate marketing program, or in their first marketing role. Understands marketing vocabulary (brand equity, performance marketing, in-house vs. agency) but has never managed a real budget under organizational pressure.
Core Pain Point
They can define marketing frameworks fluently. They have never had a CEO ask them why revenue is down. The gap between knowing marketing and leading it is not closed by more theory. It requires simulated experience under pressure.
Why Smart Starting Point
Concentrated in MBA programs where professors are the natural distribution channel. Prof. Marr's course use validated this model. Active LinkedIn users who share career content organically. Enough marketing literacy to appreciate the game's depth.
Key Assumption
This audience is primarily US-based and English-speaking. A second wave targeting international markets, particularly India and Southeast Asia where MBA marketing programs are growing rapidly, would require a different channel strategy.
Market Sizing
Total Addressable Market
9.4M
All marketing professionals on LinkedIn globally who could benefit from strategy simulation practice.
Serviceable Addressable Market
450K
US-based marketing MBA students and early-career marketers (1 to 3 years) actively seeking career development tools.
Serviceable Obtainable Market
15K
Reachable in 90 days through professor outreach at 50 MBA programs combined with Andrew Mitrak's existing LinkedIn and podcast audience.
03Positioning and Messaging
Positioning Statement
For marketing MBA students and early-career marketers preparing for their first leadership role, The CMO Game is a free marketing strategy simulator that puts you inside the organizational pressure of real marketing decisions (budget trade-offs, CEO expectations, audience dynamics) because the gap between knowing marketing and leading it is only closed by doing.
Headlines and Message Directions
01 You've studied marketing. Now survive it. Selected
Lose state
0212 months. $5M. One angry CEO. Can you make CMO?
03The gap between knowing marketing and doing marketing, closed in 20 minutes.
04Not a certificate. A global rank. The CMO Game puts your marketing strategy on a leaderboard against players worldwide.
"Mitrak's game sparks the right conversations by not giving you marketing answers, but better questions to ask, which is where real learning starts."
Elton X. Graham, CMO, Sur La Table
Prof. Marr uses The CMO Game to open his Advanced Marketing course, describing it as "a great way to break the ice in the first class." I was in that session. Andrew Mitrak came to the class in person.
Source: Andrew Mitrak, A History of Marketing ↗
Why the Leaderboard Changes the Value Proposition
Most marketing simulations end with a certificate. The CMO Game ends with a global rank. A leaderboard entry is more useful in a job interview: it shows the specific strategy you played, the revenue you generated, and your brand equity score, all benchmarked against real players worldwide. The same players appear multiple times in the top 100, each entry a different strategic bet. The learning is not in finishing. It is in coming back and doing it differently.
Top 100 players worldwide, ranked by revenue and brand equity. The same player appears multiple times with different strategies.
Source: Andrew Mitrak's launch post for The CMO Game, marketinghistory.org
What I Accepted, Edited, and Rejected from AI
AI Suggested (Rejected)
Positioned the CMO Game as "edtech" alongside LinkedIn Learning and Coursera. Messaging emphasized skill certifications and course completion. Recommended headline: "Master Marketing Strategy with The CMO Game." Treated the absence of a certificate as a product weakness.
Human Revision (Kept)
Repositioned as a strategy simulator. "Master" implies a credential. "Survive" implies pressure. Pressure is the product. The leaderboard rank is a stronger credential than any certificate: competitive, global, and conversation-starting. Accepted: the positioning template structure and speed of headline iteration.
04Lightweight GTM Plan
Marketing MBA Programs
Professors are the distribution layer. One adoption delivers the game to 30 to 60 students in a single session at zero acquisition cost. Prof. Marr's use case is a validated, replicable template. Target: marketing department chairs and professors at the top 50 US MBA programs.
Andrew Mitrak's Owned Platforms
Andrew is Senior Manager at Google Cloud AI Demand Labs with 5,960 LinkedIn followers and an active posting cadence. His podcast "A History of Marketing" features guests including Philip Kotler, April Dunford, and Guy Kawasaki. A creator employed at Google with a respected marketing podcast carries credibility no paid placement can replicate. When guests share the game with their networks, it reaches the exact audience the target player wants to impress on the leaderboard.
LinkedIn Player Sharing
The target audience already shares career-relevant content on LinkedIn. A rank-based sharing prompt converts every player into a distribution node, creating a growing organic signal that reaches the exact audience most likely to play and share again.
Launch Tactics
Tactic 1: Class Competition Format
Pitch professors not on a game, but on a structured class competition. Students play individually, then compare leaderboard rankings and debate their decisions. "Which team hit the highest revenue, and why?" generates post-game analysis that makes the learning permanent. Prof. Marr's session is the validated template. Estimated conversion: 1 in 4 professors pitched runs a competition within 30 days.
Tactic 2: Score-Sharing Prompt
At game completion, prompt players with a rank-anchored LinkedIn post: "I just hit Rank [X] in the CMO Game Hall of Fame. I chose [product] with a [positioning] strategy and generated [revenue] in 12 months. My biggest mistake: [X]. Think you can beat it? cmogame.com" Rank is more provocative than revenue. It implies an open challenge.
The win state: the screen players post to LinkedIn. The rank and revenue are the social object.

Mockup showing what the rank-based sharing prompt looks like. The rank invites a direct challenge.
90-Day Launch Sequence
Weeks 1 to 2Outreach to 20 marketing professors at target MBA programs via LinkedIn, citing Prof. Marr's use case and offering Andrew a virtual class session.
Weeks 3 to 6Andrew attends or connects with 3 to 5 class sessions run as competitions. Students compare leaderboard ranks and debate their decisions. Each session produces players who post their rank on LinkedIn.
Weeks 4 to 8Andrew features the game on his podcast. Guests with large followings (CMOs, professors) share with their networks, creating earned amplification to senior marketing audiences.
Weeks 5 to 8Track LinkedIn organic posts. Optimize the sharing prompt based on which result types drive the most engagement and challenge responses.
Weeks 9 to 12Second wave outreach to 20 additional professors using social proof from wave one adoptions. Each new adopting professor becomes a reference for the next.
KPI 1: 90-Day Target
15 courses
MBA or graduate marketing programs formally incorporating the game as a class competition. Each adoption is a multiplier: one professor equals up to 60 new players who then share their rank on LinkedIn.
KPI 2: 90-Day Target
35% replay rate
Percentage of first-time players who return for a second run within 14 days. This is a deeper signal than post counts. A 35% replay rate means players are returning to test a different strategy, which validates the leaderboard as a retention mechanic.
05AI Process and Reflection
"I'm developing a GTM snapshot for The CMO Game, a free browser-based marketing strategy simulator by Andrew Mitrak. Target audience: marketing students and early-career marketers. Creator has an active LinkedIn presence (5,960 followers) and a podcast with guests including Philip Kotler and April Dunford. No paid acquisition budget. Generate: (1) positioning statement, (2) three headlines, (3) two to three launch channels, (4) two 90-day KPIs."

⚠ AI categorized this as edtech. All recommendations followed from that wrong frame.

Positioning: "The only free marketing strategy game that lets you practice CMO-level decisions."

Headlines: "Master Marketing Strategy" / "Think Like a CMO" / "Level Up Your Marketing Career"

Channels: SEO targeting "marketing career" keywords. LinkedIn Learning-style newsletter partnerships.

KPIs: Monthly active users. Course completion rate.

✓ Three corrections changed the entire strategy.

Category reframe: Not edtech. Strategy simulator. No certificate, no modules, no completion state.

Headline: "You've studied marketing. Now survive it." Survive implies pressure. Pressure is the product.

Channels: Professor-led class competitions + Andrew's Google and podcast credibility. Not SEO.

KPIs: Course adoptions + 35% replay rate. Not MAU.

What AI Helped
Positioning template structure and fast headline iteration. Generating eight variations quickly surfaced the rank-based angle ("Not a certificate. A global rank."). AI also correctly flagged Andrew's podcast as a channel and executive endorsements as credibility signals.
What I Overrode
Audience definition and product category. AI suggested "marketing professionals and aspiring CMOs" (too broad) and categorized the game as edtech (wrong frame). The audience insight came from playing in Prof. Marr's class and noticing which moments landed hardest: budget pressure and CEO reactions, not marketing tactics.
Ethical and Accuracy Risks
AI generated an unverified market size statistic for "marketing simulation software." Removed entirely. AI treated the absence of a completion certificate as a product weakness and recommended adding one. This is wrong: the Hall of Fame leaderboard rank is a stronger credential. It is competitive, specific, global, and more conversation-starting in a job interview than any badge. AI also used "the only free tool," a superlative that cannot be verified. Softened to "one of the few free" options in the category.
